topSheets.name: Z SCORE -PRIVATE & HEAVY ASSETS
labels: "Z SCORE" -  ANALYSIS of BUSINESS FAILURE POTENTIAL & TREND
Weighting Factors Based on Mfg. Industry, Tool & Die, Excavating, etc. or any company with a high asset value.
2.91 and Above = Failure Potential Low - ( Company is in Good Shape )
The "Z-Score" bankruptcy predictor has a reported a 72% accuracy in predicting bankruptcies 2 years in advance.
1.22 & Lower = Business Failure Potential Very High

topSheets.name: COMPARATIVE ACTIVITY RATIOS
labels: Avg Payment Period      (acc Payable / Daily Exp)
Avg Collection Period      (days in a yr / AR Turn)
Annual Asset Turn    (Annual Sales / Total Assets)
Fixed Asset Turn      (Annual Sales / Fixed Assets)
Inventory Average Age    ( Days in Period / Turns)

sheets.name: Employee Questionnaire Tally
labels: Note: Any unreturned questionnaires have been discounted completely and were not counted as a YES or a NO.
RED NUMBERS ARE LESS THAN THE TARGETED % "YES" (OR MORE THAN TARGETED % "NO") FOR AN EFFECTIVE ORGANIZATION
Do you have a clear understanding of this company's goals for this year?
If so, do you feel this company is on the proper path to achieve these goals?
Are your duties and responsibilities clearly defined in written format?
Does this company have a policy/procedures manual. (employee handbook)?
Do you have a copy of this company's policy / procedures manual?
Does management consistently enforce these policies / procedures?
Does this company have a current organizational chart?
Do you feel the management structure is stable and effective?
Is it always clear to whom you report?
Have you been properly trained to do your job?
Is communication from upper management clear, accurate and timely?
Does management ask for opinions and ideas from employees?
Does management listen to and/or implement employee ideas?
Does management communicate with the employees effectively?
Are all employees always treated with dignity and respect?
Is employee morale as good as it could or should be?
Do you feel your contributions to this company are appreciated?
Is on-going or advanced training available on-site and/or off-site?
Does management handle employees concerns quickly and effectively?
Is your compensation fair relative to your duties and productivity?
Does this company have productivity incentives that are fair and effective?
Are employee performance reviews conducted on a regular schedule?
Would you recommend this company to a friend as a good place to work?
Do you believe you will be employed here long term? (next 10yrs)

sheets.name: FIXED BUSINESS VAL APPROX
labels: Approximate Business Value Range ----- Increased Sales and Increased Profits
Projected Percentage of Net Operating Profit
Projected Net Operating Profit With Increased Sales
Average Interest For The Past 3 Years
Average Depreciation & Amortization For The Past 3 Years
EBITDA = Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation, & Amortization
* Add Net Asset Value for Total Approximate Business Value
NOTE: If "EBITDA" is Less Than Zero, use "Book Value" for Value of Business, which is the value of NET ASSETS
PLEASE NOTE - THIS CHART IS MEANT ONLY TO SHOW HOW REVENUE AND EARNINGS CAN AFFECT APPROXIMATE BUSINESS VALUE RANGES.
IT IS NOT IMPLIED OR MEANT TO BE INTERPRETED AS THE ACTUAL VALUE OF A COMPANY NOW OR IN THE FUTURE FOR ANY PURPOSE WHATSOEVER.

sheets.name: Z SCORE -PRIVATE & LIGHT ASSETS
labels: "Z SCORE" -  ANALYSIS of BUSINESS FAILURE POTENTIAL & TREND
Weighting Factors Based on Service Industry, Retail, Non Manuf. companies, etc. with low asset value.
2.91 and Above = Failure Potential Low - ( Company is in Good Shape )
The "Z-Score" bankruptcy predictor has a reported 72% accuracy in predicting bankruptcies 2 years in advance.
1.22 & Lower = Business Failure Potential Very High

sheets.name: Z SCORE -PRIVATE & HEAVY ASSETS
labels: "Z SCORE" -  ANALYSIS of BUSINESS FAILURE POTENTIAL & TREND
Weighting Factors Based on Mfg. Industry, Tool & Die, Excavating, etc. or any company with a high asset value.
2.91 and Above = Failure Potential Low - ( Company is in Good Shape )
The "Z-Score" bankruptcy predictor has a reported a 72% accuracy in predicting bankruptcies 2 years in advance.
1.22 & Lower = Business Failure Potential Very High

sheets.name: Employee Questionnaire PG1
labels: Please return directly to the Senior Analyst
In order to assess the  current state of the business, your input is crucial. Every company is comprised of the sum total of its parts. Your opinions and
suggestions are a vital part of moving this company forward and making it the very best it can be. Please answer the questions below as fully as you
can. Your answers will be held in the strictest confidence. The owner (s) will be informed as to what has been said, but will never be told who said exactly
what. No employee, manager or principal of the company will ever be shown this completed form UNDER ANY CIRCUMSTANCES. Keep in mind there
are no right or wrong answers. You may include your name, but it is not required. Thank you for your assistance.
How long have you been with this company? __________________________________
Are your working conditions conducive to your performing to the best of your abilities?  If not, why not? ___________
Do you have all the tools or training necessary to perform everything expected of you? _________________________
Do you believe you are in the position that best uses your skills, experience and knowledge?    If not, why not?
Do employees ever work overtime when it was not necessary to the job? ______________________________________
When is the last time you received a formal review?__________________________________
When is the last time you received a raise or bonus?_________________________________
If you owned this company, would you allow everyone employed here today to work for you? ______________________
If you owned this company, what are the first few changes you would make and why? _____________________________
If my schedule allows do you have something in particular you would like to speak about with me in person?
Please use reverse side if necessary

sheets.name: Employee Questionnaire PG2
labels: Please return directly to the Senior Analyst
Respond "yes" or "no" which best answers the questions.
Do you have a clear understanding of this company's goals for this year?
If so, do you feel this company is on the proper path to achieve these goals?
Are your duties and responsibilities clearly defined in written format?
Does this company have a policy/procedures manual. (employee handbook)?
Do you have a copy of this company's policy / procedures manual?
Does management consistently enforce these policies / procedures?
Does this company have a current organizational chart?
Do you feel the management structure is stable and effective?
Is it always clear to whom you report?
Have you been properly trained to do your job?
Is communication from upper management clear, accurate and timely?
Does management ask for opinions and ideas from employees?
Does management listen to and/or implement employee ideas?
Does management communicate with the employees effectively?
Are all employees always treated with dignity and respect?
Is employee morale as good as it could or should be?
Do you feel your contributions to this company are appreciated?
Is on-going or advanced training available on-site and/or off-site?
Does management handle employees concerns quickly and effectively?
Is your compensation fair relative to your duties and productivity?
Does this company have productivity incentives that are fair and effective?
Are employee performance reviews conducted on a regular schedule?
Would you recommend this company to a friend as a good place to work?
Do you believe you will be employed here long term? (next 10yrs)
WE THANK YOU FOR YOUR COOPERATION AND HONEST FEEDBACK WITH THIS SURVEY !!

sheets.name: Ratios Introduction
labels: This section of reports reflect 11 important financial ratios. These ratios are usually thought of as belonging to four basic categories: profitability ratios, liquidity ratios, activity ratios, and leverage ratios:
This is by no means an exhaustive list of the ratios that have been developed to help analyze a company's financial position and the way that it conducts business. It is, however, representative.

The issue of liquidity, as you might expect, concerns creditors. Liquidity is a company's ability to meet its debts as they come due. A company may have considerable total assets, but if those assets are difficult to convert to cash it is possible that the company might be unable to pay its creditors in a timely fashion. Creditors want their loans to be paid in the medium of cash, not in a medium such as inventory or factory equipment.

There are various ratios that can give you insight into how well a company manages its operating and sales activities. One primary goal-perhaps, the primary goal-of these activities is to produce income through effective use of its resources. Two ways to measure this effectiveness are the Average Collection Period and the Inventory Turnover rate.

The term "leverage" means the purchase of assets with borrowed money. Suppose that your company retails office supplies. When you receive an order for business cards, you pay one of your suppliers 50 percent of the revenue to print them for you. This is a variable cost: the more you sell, the greater your cost.

But if you purchase the necessary printing equipment, you could make the business cards yourself. So doing would turn a variable cost into a fixed cost: no matter how many cards you sell, the cost of printing them is fixed at however much you paid for the printing equipment. The more cards you sell, the greater your profit margin. This effect is termed operating leverage.

If you borrow money to acquire the printing equipment, you are using another type of leverage, termed financial leverage. The cost is still fixed at however much money you must pay, at regular intervals, to retire the loan. Again, the more cards you sell, the greater your profit margin. But if you do not sell enough cards to cover the loan payment, you could lose money. In that case, it might be difficult to find funds either to make the loan payments or to cover your other expenses. Your credit rating might fall, making it more costly for you to borrow other money.

Leverage is a financial tool that accelerates changes in income, both positive and negative. A company's creditors and investors are interested in how much leverage has been used to acquire assets. From the standpoint of creditors, a high degree of leverage represents risk because the company might not be able to repay a loan. From the investors' standpoint, if the return on assets is less than the cost of borrowing money to acquire assets, then the investment is unattractive. The investor could obtain a better return in different ways-one way would be to loan funds rather than to invest them in the company.

These reports have some of the financial ratios that are important to understanding how, and how well, a company conducts its business. There are variations on virtually every ratio discussed here, and there are ratios that were not covered at all, but their principal forms follow the formulas illustrated.

Only occasionally can you calculate one of these indicators and gain immediate insight into a business operation. More frequently, it is necessary to know the sort of business that a company conducts, because the marketplace imposes different demands on different lines of business. Furthermore, you can usually understand one ratio by considering it in the context of another ratio (the debt ratio and the return on assets is a good example of one ratio providing the context for another).

Keep in mind that it's important to evaluate a financial ratio in terms of its trend over time, of a standard such as an industry average, and in light of other ratios that describe the company's operations and financial structure.
